coredata.dc:title: High-flux nanofiltration membranes prepared with β-cyclodextrin and graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Jing
Shen, Jianliang
Zhang, Runnan
Wang, Fei
Liang, Shuwei
You, Xinda
Yu, Qianqian
Hao, Yufeng
Su, Yanlei
Jiang, Zhongyi
coredata.dc:description: In this study, high flux β-CD/GQDs nanofiltration (NF) membranes were prepared by interfacial polymerization with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) as the aqueous monomer, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the additive and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) as the organic monomer. The addition of GQDs not only modulated the membrane surface properties, but also led to the formation of a loose selective layer due to the nonspecific hydrogen bonding interactions between GQDs and β-CD. The pure water flux of the β-CD/GQDs NF membranes increased from 122.2 to 474.7 L/m2h (at the pressure of 0.1 MPa) when the concentration of GQDs increased from 0 to 0.5 wt%, almost 3.9 times higher than that of the pristine β-CD membrane. The dye rejection of Eriochrome Black T and Congo Red essentially remained above 93.0%. The β-CD/GQDs NF membranes with superior filtration performance and excellent chlorine resistance hold great promise in dye wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
β-Cyclodextrin
Interfacial polymerization
Loose nanofiltration membrane
High-flux

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the structure of β-CD/GQDs NF membranes.
(a) FTIR spectra of GQDs, β-CD and β-CD/GQDs; (b) Size distribution and photoluminescent property of β-CD and β-CD/GQDs aqueous solution.
(a) Surface and (b) cross-sectional morphology images of 0#, 1#, 3#, 5# and 6# membranes; (c) Root-mean-square roughness (Rms) of 0#, 1#, 3#, 5# and 6# membranes.
Scheme of reaction mechanism among β-CD, GQDs, and TMC.

(a) Pore width distribution analyzed by BET N2 adsorption isotherm using DFT model respectively; (b) rejection of dyes (Methyl Orange, Sunset Yellow, Acid Fuchsin, Bromocresol Green, Methyl Blue, Evans Blue) with different molecular weight through β-CD/GQDs NF membrane. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

NF performance of β-CD and β-CD/GQDs NF membranes. (a) Pure water flux and dyes rejection of 0# to 6# membranes (the concentration of β-CD was fixed at 1.8 wt%); (b) Pure water flux and dyes rejection of 7# to 12# membranes (the concentration of GQDs was fixed at 0.3 wt%).
The performance comparison of the membranes prepared in this work and other membranes prepared in literatures.
Chlorine resistance property of (a) β-CD/GQDs-0.1 (1#) and (b) β-CD/GQDs-0.3 (3#) NF membrane.
β-CD/GQDs NF membranes prepared by regulating aqueous phase monomer concentrations.

head.ce:title: High-flux nanofiltration membranes prepared with β-cyclodextrin and graphene quantum dots

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, high flux β-CD/GQDs nanofiltration (NF) membranes were prepared by interfacial polymerization with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) as the aqueous monomer, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the additive and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) as the organic monomer. The addition of GQDs not only modulated the membrane surface properties, but also led to the formation of a loose selective layer due to the nonspecific hydrogen bonding interactions between GQDs and β-CD. The pure water flux of the β-CD/GQDs NF membranes increased from 122.2 to 474.7 L/m2h (at the pressure of 0.1 MPa) when the concentration of GQDs increased from 0 to 0.5 wt%, almost 3.9 times higher than that of the pristine β-CD membrane. The dye rejection of Eriochrome Black T and Congo Red essentially remained above 93.0%. The β-CD/GQDs NF membranes with superior filtration performance and excellent chlorine resistance hold great promise in dye wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: The shortage and pollution of water resources have posed a threat to human life, and textile wastewater accounts for 17–20% of industrial water pollution [1,2]. Recently, membrane technology has played an important role in water purification and recycling [3]. Thin film composite nanofiltration (TFC NF) membranes, which are usually prepared by interfacial polymerization (IP), have attracted extensive attention in textile wastewater treatment primarily due to their excellent separation performance and low energy consumption [4–6]. To ensure the efficiency during the operation process, TFC NF membranes with high flux, good rejection and chemical stability are urgently needed.

β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) is a circular oligomer with the molecular size of 1–2 nm, which is composed of seven glucose units. It has a truncated cone shape with hydrophilic hydroxyl groups at the external side and hydrophobic methylene groups at the internal side. β-CD is commonly used in water treatment fields because of its unique hydrophobic cavities and host-gust interactions [7]. In recent years, β-CD has been employed as the building block instead of traditional amide monomers to react with acyl chloride groups. A selective polyester layer without defects was formed and the resultant NF membrane manifested the superior performance (high flux, solvent resistance and chlorine resistance). First, β-CD is rich in active hydroxyl groups that can be crosslinked by trimesoyl chloride (TMC). Second, the size of β-CD cavity (6.0–7.8 Å) is larger than the kinetic diameter of water molecules, introducing additional water transport channels to the selective layer. Moreover, the residual hydroxyl groups can improve the hydrophilicity of the NF membrane surfaces [8–10]. In our previous work, we found that the flux of β-CD NF membrane was obviously increased with the increase of β-CD concentration [10]. When the concentration of β-CD was high, it was easy to aggregate in water owing to the low solubility, leading to the generation of defects within the selective layer [11]. Therefore, it was difficult to further improve the membrane permeability without sacrificing the membrane rejection.

To further improve the permeability of membrane, one facile way is to incorporate nanomaterials into the selective layer. Nanomaterials include nanoparticles [12–14], zeolites [15–17], graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets [18–21] and others with regular and permanent channels such as carbon nanotube (CNT) [22–24], metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [25,26] and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [27–29]. They all effectively improved the permeability of NF membranes by regulating the surface, channel and interface structure of the selective layer. In addition, the nanomaterials were generally incorporated into polyamide (PA) selective layer formed by amine and acyl chloride monomers through IP process, which largely determined the membrane performance [30]. Nevertheless, the poor compatibility between incorporated nanomaterials and polymers may cause the formation of defects in selective layer leading to low selectivity.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial derived from GO. It has a zero-dimensional structure and its size is smaller than that of GO lakes. GQDs owns most of the properties of GO, which provides hydrophobic channels with low friction and tortuosity for water transport [31]. GQDs with hydrophilic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups exhibits good dispersion and compatibility with other kinds of materials [32,33]. GQDs has been incorporated into PA membranes to significantly enhance membrane permeability without sacrificing membrane selectivity [33–37]. GQDs was incorporated within the tannic acid selective layer to improve the NF membrane filtration performance [31]. Furthermore, Li et al. prepared solvent-resistant NF membranes by embedding GQDs into the selective layer [38]. Notably, Bi et al. found that GQDs as aqueous phase monomer reacted with trimesoyl chloride (TMC) in the pores of ultrafiltration membrane and the prepared NF membrane exhibited superior separation performance, which is different from the traditional IP process [39]. Moreover, according to the previous studies [40,41], stable hydrogen bonds existed between the oxygen-containing groups of GQDs and the hydroxyl groups of β-CD, which may enhance the compatibility between GQDs and the polyester network and prevent the formation of defects in the selective layer.

In this study, GQDs was incorporated into β-CD polyester membranes by IP, and the synergistic effect between GQDs and β-CD endowed the β-CD/GQDs NF membranes with excellent performance. β-CD has abundant hydroxyl groups and is easy to form a defect-free selective layer. GQDs with hydrophilic functional groups can be well dispersed in aqueous phase. The similar size of β-CD and GQDs promoted mutual compatibility, and the hydrogen bonds between β-CD and GQDs generated the synergistic effect in membrane structure formation and manipulation. Meanwhile, β-CD with hydrophobic cavity and GQDs with hydrophobic channel jointly accelerated water transport. The permeability and selectivity of β-CD/GQDs NF membranes were tested and their surface property was evaluated in detail. This study may open a new avenue to the preparation of NF membranes with high flux by combining two kinds of nanomaterials into the selective layer.

Membrane preparation materials include polyether sulfone (PES) (Ultrason E6020P), polyethylene glycol (PEG, Mw = 2000 Da), N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and citric acid (CA). Materials for membrane performance test include Eriochrome Black T (C20H12N3NaO7S, Mw = 461 Da), Congo Red (C32H22N6Na2O6S2, Mw = 696 Da), Methyl Blue (C37H27N3Na2O9S3, Mw = 799 Da), Alcian Blue (C56H68Cl4CuN16S4, Mw = 1299 Da), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) and potassium bromide (KBr). PES was supplied by BASF Co. (Germany). β-CD was supplied by Jiangtian Chemical Technology Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Chemicals mentioned above were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China).

The bottom-up method was used to prepare GQDs as described in literature [42]. CA (2 g) was loaded in the porcelain crucible and reacted in the oven at the temperature of 200 °C for 15 min. CA was liquated and pyrolyzed to synthesize GQDs by the process of incomplete carbonization. The synthesized GQDs with orange colour was directly dissolved in deionized (DI) water to prepare solutions of different concentrations.

PES membranes were prepared by non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS) and chosen as the support. The percentage of PES, PEG and DMF were 16, 16 and 68 wt%, respectively. The specific preparation process was described in our previous work [43]. PES membranes were immersed in DI water for 48 h before used.

β-CD and GQDs were added to DI water to obtain the aqueous phase, and TMC was dissolved in n-heptane to obtain the organic phase. First, PES support was immersed in aqueous phase for 30 min, and the residual water on support surface was slightly drained with filter paper. Then, the support loaded with aqueous phase monomer was immersed in organic phase for 10 min to conduct IP reaction. Finally, the prepared membrane was taken out from the organic solution and was then heated in an oven at 60 °C for 15 min. The heating treatment process could strengthen the polymerization of β-CD/GQDs and TMC and accelerate the evaporation of n-heptane. A control IP process was conducted to verify the formation of polyester film from β-CD and TMC (Fig. S2). The schematic diagram of the structure of β-CD/GQDs NF membranes was exhibited in Fig. 1 . The concentration of β-CD and GQDs were regulated during the membrane preparation process, while the concentration of TMC is fixed. Table 1 showed β-CD/GQDs NF membranes fabricated with different concentrations of β-CD and GQDs.

The morphology and size of GQDs were investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM 2100F). The size distribution of β-CD and β-CD/GQDs aqueous solutions were tested by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Malvern, MAL1131484). The chemical structure of monomers and membrane surface was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Nicolet 560). The wavenumber range was chosen as 500–3000 cm−1. The solid powder of β-CD and GQDs were tested by pressed-disk technique and β-CD/GQDs aqueous solution was dropped onto KBr samples for characterization.

The field-emission scanning electronic microscopy system (SEM, Nova Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA) was used to characterize the morphology characteristics of membrane surface and cross-section. The root-mean-square (RMS) roughness and surface topography of membrane surface (5 μm × 5 μm) were analyzed by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM, Park Systems XE-70). The pore distribution of the prepared NF membrane was explored using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) nitrogen absorption (BELSOPR-Max, Microtrac BET, Japan) and analyzed by Horvath-Kawazoe (HK) and density functional theory (DFT) method.

The permeation and separation performance of membranes were measured in a dead-end stirred filtration apparatus. The steel cylinder containing nitrogen gas acts as pressure source. A buffer tank of 1.0 L was filled with DI water. The membrane installed in the filter device was pressed at 0.15 MPa for 30 min until the flux was essentially unchanged. The volume of permeated DI water (V, L), the effective membrane area (A, m2) and the permeation time (ΔT, h) were recorded. Pure water flux (Jw ) was measured at 0.1 MPa and calculated by the following equation:
(1)
J w
=
V
A Δ T

The dye solution (0.1 g/L) of Eriochrome Black T, Congo Red, Alcian Blue and Methyl Blue and the Na2SO4 solution (1.0 g/L) were used to measure the separation performance. C
p and C
f represented the concentrations of dye and salt solutions in the permeate and feed, respectively. They were determined by the ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi UV-2800) and electric conductivity meter (DDS-11A, Shanghai, China), respectively. The rejection ratios (R) were calculated by the equation:
(2)
R =
C f
−
C p
C f
× 100 %

The chemical stability is one of the most important properties of NF membranes. The resultant β-CD/GQDs NF membranes were immersed into sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) solution (10000 ppm, pH = 11.5) for 120 h. The pure water flux and dyes rejection were tested every 24 h to evaluate chemical stability. Meanwhile, the NaClO solution was changed every 24 h to ensure the high concentration.

The interaction of GQDs and β-CD was first explored. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the FTIR spectra of GQDs, β-CD and β-CD/GQDs were tested respectively, which verified the existence of hydrogen bonds after GQDs was added into β-CD solution. Peaks at 1029 cm−1 was ascribed to the C–O–C of β-CD and GQDs was characterized with the absorbance signal at 1712 cm−1 (CO) and 1642 cm−1 (CC). The characteristic peaks of β-CD and GQDs were observed in the spectra of β-CD/GQDs. Peaks of CO and CC shifted to 1719 and 1638 cm−1, indicating the existence of hydrogen bonds between GQDs with oxygen-containing groups and β-CD with hydroxyl groups [41].

Particle size of GQDs in β-CD aqueous solution was obtained from dynamic light scattering (Fig. 2(b)). It slightly increased from 1.3 to 2.3 nm with the concentration of GQDs increased from 0 to 0.5 wt%. The size distribution and photoluminescent property [42] all proved that GQDs were completely dispersed in β-CD aqueous solution. The existence of hydrogen bonding between β-CD and GQDs prevented the aggregation, leading to the good dispersion in aqueous solution for both of them.

β-CD as aqueous phase monomer and GQDs as additive were used to fabricate separation layer by IP. SEM and AFM analysis were utilized to characterize the structure of the prepared membranes. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), both β-CD and β-CD/GQDs NF membranes presented smooth membrane surface. AFM images (Fig. 3(c)) further proved that the addition of some zero-dimensional GQDs have caused a little decrease of Rms. Furthermore, no obvious aggregation phenomenon could be observed on the membrane surfaces due to the well-dispersed property of GQDs in polymer matrix [31]. It was worth noting that the apparent defects were observed when the addition of GQDs was 0.6 wt%. This could be caused by the aggregation of β-CD and GQDs at higher concentration and thus affected the formation of defect-free selective layer during IP process. The cross-sectional morphologies were shown in Fig. 3(b), and the selective layer of all the membranes were observed obviously. The thickness of β-CD/GQDs selective layer was slightly increased with the increase of GQDs concentration from 0 to 0.6% (Fig. S3).

Fig. 4 showed the probable reaction mechanism of β-CD and GQDs in the IP process. GQDs was effectively incorporated into membrane surface as shown in Fig. S4. When a certain amount of GQDs was doped into aqueous phase, it interacted with β-CD by hydrogen bonds, where the similar molecular size of them also made them synergistically play important role in improving the interface property. The hydrogen bonds between β-CD and GQDs made them congruously diffuse to the oil/water interface, thereby GQDs was uniformly acted on β-CD selective layer. And the agglomeration of β-CD could be effectively alleviated by the hydrogen bonds existing between β-CD and GQDs.

According to Freger's kinetic model [44], the thickness of the selective layer could be modulated by the diffusivity of aqueous monomers. The hydrogen bonding between GQDs with oxygen-containing groups and β-CD with hydroxyl groups and the steric hindrance of zero-dimensional GQDs all decreased the diffusion rate of β-CD [31,41]. However, a little increased thickness of selective layer (Fig. S3) was not in line with the theory. This could be explained by the fact that the addition of GQDs would result in a loose selective layer, which would accelerate the diffusion rate of β-CD monomers in the newly formed layer, ultimately increasing the thickness of the selective layer slightly.

To evaluate the effects of GQDs on the performance of β-CD NF membranes, the permeability and selectivity of the prepared membranes were tested as shown in Fig. 6. Water flux of the resultant membranes increased linearly from 122.2 to 474.7 L/m2h with the increase of GQDs concentration from 0 to 0.5 wt%. Meanwhile, the rejection of Eriochrome Black T and Congo Red remained above 93.0% and the rejection of Methyl Blue and Alcian Blue remained above 75.5 and 88.0% respectively. Further increasing GQDs concentration to 0.6 wt%, the interface gaps or voids appeared in selective layer as shown in the SEM images of surface morphology (Fig. 3), which resulted in higher flux with lower rejection. The flux increased notably with the incorporation of GQDs, which could be explained by the following aspects. GQDs with similar properties to GO flakes and quantum size were effectively incorporated into selective layer, providing hydrophobic channels with low friction and tortuosity for water transport [31]. The hydrogen bonding between β-CD and GQDs would restrict the diffusion rate in aqueous phase, thus further limiting the rate of interfacial polymerization. This made selective layer more loose and accelerated the speed of water transport. Above all, incorporating GQDs with well-dispersed properties in selective layer made it more loose but not increased the pore size as shown in Fig. 5.

When the concentration of GQDs was fixed, the water flux of the membranes also increased from 107.0 to 410.2 L/m2h with the concentration of β-CD increased from 1.0 to 2.5 wt%. This was mainly due to that β-CD as aqueous monomers could construct a loose selective layer and provide transport channels for water molecules [10]. Compared with increasing the concentration of GQDs, the water flux of resultant membrane improved indistinctively when the concentration of β-CD increased. And the rejection of Methyl Blue decreased to 75.0% when the concentration of β-CD was 2.5 wt%. The poor solubility and aggregation of β-CD at high concentration lead to the poor performance of the prepared membranes [46]. According to above experimental results, the membranes with superior performance were prepared in the synergistic effect between GQDs and β-CD.

The selectivity of β-CD and β-CD/GQDs NF membranes were explored in detail. For β-CD/GQDs-0.3 NF membrane (3#), the rejection of Eriochrome Black T, Congo Red, Alcian Blue and Methyl Blue was about 97.1, 96.4, 90.9, and 86.2%, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). Further increasing GQDs concentration to 0.6%, the rejection of Methyl Blue decreased clearly to 68.4% and the rejection of the other three dyes changed indistinctively. The rejection of Na2SO4 was below 20.0% for all the prepared membranes. Structural information of model organic dyes used in this work were listed in Fig. S11. Eriochrome Black T with smaller molecular weight but strong negative electric charge was effectively repulsed away from the negative surface (Fig. S6) of β-CD/GQDs NF membranes [47], which was coincident with Donnan effect. The dyes could be effectively rejected by NF membranes according to the mechanism of Donnan effect and molecular sieving. Eriochrome Black T with strong negative charge made it rejected more effective. For the rejection of the other three dyes, the effect of molecular sieving mechanism is more significant. Although the molecular weight of Congo Red (Mw = 696 Da) is slightly smaller than Methyl Blue (Mw = 799 Da), the rejection of the former is significantly higher than the latter. This could be caused by the aggregation properties of Congo Red in aqueous solution, which made them form supramolecular systems [45,48]. The resultant membranes showed better rejection for Alcian Blue with larger molecular weight, but poor rejection for Methyl Blue. The UV–vis absorption spectra indicates that the molecular sieving and Donnan effect rather than adsorption play a leading role for dye separation (Fig. S9). As shown in Fig. 7 , the performance of the NF membranes prepared in this work were compared to that of NF membranes reported in literatures. The dyes rejections were listed in Table S2 which was chosen to support the comparison in Fig. 7. It was obviously that the membranes prepared by the synergetic effect of β-CD and GQDs in this work possessed high flux and comparable dye rejection.

Chlorine resistance is necessary for NF membranes, for NaClO cleaning is a widespread and effective way for membrane fouling. The chlorine resistance of resultant β-CD/GQDs-0.1 (1#) and β-CD/GQDs-0.3 (3#) NF membranes was evaluated as shown in Fig. 8 . After 120 h immersion of NaClO solution (10000 ppm, pH = 11.5), the rejection of β-CD/GQDs-0.1 and β-CD/GQDs-0.3 NF membranes remained essentially unchanged, while the flux of β-CD/GQDs-0.1 and β-CD/GQDs-0.3 NF membranes slightly increased to 6.7 and 5.6%, respectively. Compared to pure β-CD membranes, β-CD/GQDs NF membranes also showed superior chlorine resistance due to the absence of active chlorination reaction sites in polyester selective layer [10]. In addition, the incorporation of GQDs also improved the chlorine resistance property. The hydrogen bonding between GQDs and the β-CD made the polymer layer more compact and inhibited the replacement of amidic hydrogens [34]. The antifouling experiment for dye solution (1.0 g/L) also proved the superior antifouling performance of β-CD/GQDs NF membranes (Fig. S10).

In summary, novel TFC NF membranes were prepared by IP process where β-CD and GQDs interacted by hydrogen bonding and jointly participated in the polymerization reaction. The incorporation of GQDs significantly increased the pure water flux of the resultant NF membranes, and the rejection of Eriochrome Black T and Congo Red remained almost unchanged. The significantly increased permeability was attributed to that the synergistic effect of β-CD and GQDs, enabling the formation of a loose and hydrophilic selective layer. Moreover, the β-CD/GQDs NF membranes preserved the excellent chlorine resistance characteristics of pristine β-CD NF membranes. In brief, the β-CD/GQDs NF membranes exhibited the great potential in dyes purification owing to their high flux, good dye rejection, superior antifouling ability and chlorine resistance.

Jing Xue: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Jianliang Shen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing–review& editing. Runnan Zhang: Formal analysis. Fei Wang: Formal analysis. Shuwei Liang: Investigation. Xinda You: Data curation. Qianqian Yu: Data curation. Yufeng Hao: Data curation. Yanlei Su: Supervision. Zhongyi Jiang: Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any conflict or interest existed in the submission of this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878217).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Treatment of textile wastewater with membrane bioreactor: a critical review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Jegatheesan, B. Pramanik, J. Chen, D. Navaratna, C. Chang, L. Shu, Treatment of textile wastewater with membrane bioreactor: A critical review, Bioresour. Technol. , 204 (2016) 202-212.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Science and technology for water purification in the coming decades

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shannon, P. Bohn, M. Elimelech, J. Georgiadis, B. Marinas, A. Mayes, Science and technology for water purification in the coming decades, Nature, 452 (2008) 301-310.
T. Peters, Membrane Technology for Water Treatment, Chemical Engineering & Technology, 33 (2010) 1233-1240.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymeric nanofiltration membranes for textile dye wastewater treatment: preparation, performance evaluation, transport modelling, and fouling control - a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Lau, A. Ismail, Polymeric nanofiltration membranes for textile dye wastewater treatment: Preparation, performance evaluation, transport modelling, and fouling control - a review, Desalination, 245 (2009) 321-348.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanofiltration membranes review: recent advances and future prospects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Mohammad, Y. Teow, W. Ang, Y. Chung, D. Oatley-Radcliffe, N. Hilal, Nanofiltration membranes review: Recent advances and future prospects, Desalination, 356 (2015) 226-254.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanofiltration membranes and processes: a review of research trends over the past decade

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Oatley-Radcliffe, M. Walters, T. Ainscough, P. Williams, A. Mohammad, N. Hilal, Nanofiltration membranes and processes: A review of research trends over the past decade, Journal of Water Process Engineering, 19 (2017) 164-171.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanosponge cyclodextrin polyurethanes and their modification with nanomaterials for the removal of pollutants from waste water: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Leudjo Taka, K. Pillay, X. Yangkou Mbianda, Nanosponge cyclodextrin polyurethanes and their modification with nanomaterials for the removal of pollutants from waste water: A review, Carbohydr. Polym. , 159 (2017) 94-107.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cyclodextrin films with fast solvent transport and shape-selective permeability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Villalobos, T. Huang, K. Peinemann, Cyclodextrin Films with Fast Solvent Transport and Shape-Selective Permeability, Adv. Mater. , 29 (2017).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Precise molecular sieving architectures with Janus pathways for both polar and nonpolar molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, D. Hua, Y. Zhang, S. Japip, T. Chung, Precise Molecular Sieving Architectures with Janus Pathways for Both Polar and Nonpolar Molecules, Adv. Mater. , 30 (2018).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chlorine-resistant polyester thin film composite nanofiltration membranes prepared with β-cyclodextrin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xue, Z. Jiao, R. Bi, R. Zhang, X. You, F. Wang, L. Zhou, Y. Su, Z. Jiang, Chlorine-resistant polyester thin film composite nanofiltration membranes prepared with β-cyclodextrin, J. Membr. Sci. , 584 (2019) 282-289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular dynamics of cyclodextrins in water solutions from NMR deuterium relaxation: implications for cyclodextrin aggregation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Valente, R. Carvalho, D. Murtinho, O. Soderman, Molecular Dynamics of Cyclodextrins in Water Solutions from NMR Deuterium Relaxation: Implications for Cyclodextrin Aggregation, Langmuir, 33 (2017) 8233-8238.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoparticles immobilized on thin film composite polyamide membrane: characterization, nanofiltration, antifouling properties, Polym
